# BindPort Full LLM Context

> BindPort coordinates local development ports, route metadata, generated
> integration files, dashboard visibility, and trusted lifecycle hooks without
> becoming the user's proxy, process manager, DNS layer, or secret manager.

BindPort is for local development on Linux and macOS. It wraps commands,
assigns stable ports from a configured range, records project/service/worktree
ownership in a local SQLite registry, exposes status through CLI JSON and a
dashboard, renders text files for existing tools such as Traefik, and runs
locally trusted hooks when lifecycle events happen.

BindPort does not bind `80` or `443`, install certificates, mutate DNS, edit
`/etc/hosts`, install a root daemon, or kill active processes by default.

## Core Commands

- `bindport -- <command>` runs a one-off command with an assigned `PORT`.
- `bindport run [service] [-- <command>]` runs a configured service or one-off
  override.
- `bindport reserve [service]` holds a port for an external process.
- `bindport reserve --all` idempotently prepares every named service in the
  discovered project and current worktree without starting children or owning
  sockets. All new reservations commit together or not at all.
- `bindport release [service|port]` releases a reserved port.
- `bindport status [--json]` reads registry state.
- `bindport list [--json]` groups registry services by project.
- `bindport open [service]` prints or opens the best URL.
- `bindport port <service> [--project PROJECT]` prints only the decimal port and
  newline for one active or reserved service in the current worktree scope.
  Missing, stopped, stale, and ambiguous matches fail.
- `bindport clean` removes stopped and stale registry entries.
- `bindport init` creates project or user fallback config.
- `bindport config explain` explains config discovery and identity sources.
- `bindport config validate` validates config.
- `bindport doctor` checks local diagnostics.
- `bindport doctor outputs` validates output setup, target hosts, output roots,
  ownership rows, and hook trust without writing files.
- `bindport dashboard serve|start|status|stop` operates the dashboard.
- `bindport render [output]` renders configured output files. Use
  `--verbose` for manual render diagnostics.
- `bindport templates list|show|export` inspects output templates.
- `bindport hooks status|trust|deny|reset` manages hook trust.

## Configuration

Project config files are `.bindport.toml`, `.bindport.json`, or
`.bindport.yaml`. Machine-local overrides live beside the project config as
`.bindport.local.*` or `bindport.local.*` and should stay untracked. User
fallback config is optional and lives under `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/bindport/` or
`~/.config/bindport/`.

Runtime identity precedence is CLI service argument, environment variables,
project config plus local overrides, then inference from package metadata,
workspace roots, git worktree, and command name.

The complete known v1-candidate shape is in `docs/config.schema.json` and its
stability policy is in `docs/reference/config-stability.md`. `default_range` is
the only supported port-range key. Unknown top-level keys are reported as
unknown. No config keys are currently deprecated, so `config validate` emits no
deprecation warnings.

Service config supports `name`, `path`, `command`, `args`, `env`, `hostname`,
`route_url`, and `health_url`. Own-service template placeholders include
`{port}`, `{host}`, `{url}`, `{project}`, `{service}`, `{hostname}`,
`{route_url}`, `{health_url}`, `{branch}`, `{branch_label}`, `{git_branch}`,
`{worktree}`, `{worktree_label}`, and `{worktree_hash}`.

Configured service `env`, `command`, and `args` additionally support exactly
`{services.<name>.<field>}` for `port`, `host`, `url`, `hostname`, `route_url`,
and `health_url`. References resolve one active/reserved registry snapshot in
the current project and exact worktree and fail before spawn for missing,
stopped, stale, ambiguous, or unavailable metadata. They do not apply to CLI,
route metadata, outputs, or hooks. Port assignment does not imply readiness;
BindPort does not start, order, supervise, or wait for dependencies.

## Registry State

Registry states are `active`, `reserved`, `stopped`, and `stale`. The registry
is local state stored under `$XDG_STATE_HOME/bindport/registry.sqlite` or
`~/.local/state/bindport/registry.sqlite`.

Agents and scripts should use `bindport port <service>` for exact decimal port
output, `bindport status --json` for normal registry detail, or
`bindport list --json` for grouped project/service inventory instead of parsing
human output or hardcoding ports. Port lookup considers only active or reserved
services in the selected project and current worktree. Use
`bindport registry export` for debug/backup snapshots that need raw lease, run,
output ownership, and render state rows.

`status --json` is frozen as schema `1.0`; its complete contract is
`docs/status.schema.json`. Existing fields, JSON kinds, nullability, meanings,
and closed enum sets remain compatible through v1. Additive object fields may
appear, so consumers ignore unknown fields and do not rely on object or array
ordering. This version is separate from registry `user_version`, export, list,
and config schemas.

The SQLite registry currently uses `user_version = 9` and automatically
upgrades tagged schemas from v0.1.0 through v0.7.0 in one transaction. A
supported upgrade preserves active/reserved identity and ports plus available
run, route, health, and output state. Newer versions are rejected without a
rewrite; failures roll back. No automatic backup, downgrade, or arbitrary data
recovery is promised. Preserve a failing registry and follow
`docs/reference/registry-migrations.md`.

## Outputs And Templates

Outputs render text files from the latest registry snapshot. Most templates
render one file per route; `bindport-haproxy` and `bindport-json-snapshot`
render one aggregate file for the whole route snapshot. Template lookup checks
project templates, global templates, and built-ins. The built-in templates are
`bindport-traefik`, `bindport-caddy`, `bindport-nginx`, `bindport-haproxy`,
`bindport-json-snapshot`, and `bindport-env-local`. Per-route templates receive
`snapshot.generated_at`, `snapshot.route_count`, `route.*`, `output.*`, and
user-provided `vars.*`.

Output roots must be relative to the config file and must not contain `..`.
BindPort tracks output ownership in the registry and refuses to overwrite
unowned or externally modified files during normal render. Ownership is scoped
to the resolved output root and config root, so separate worktrees can render
the same output and route without overwriting each other's registry row.
Legacy unscoped rows are considered only when their path is inside the current
output root. `bindport render --diff` previews content changes against
DB-owned files without writing files or updating ownership rows.
`bindport render --repair` reconciles DB-owned files and can adopt
content-identical planned files whose ownership row was lost. Files with
different content are never adopted.
`bindport doctor outputs` also reports foreign or stale ownership rows from
other output roots or worktrees as diagnostics.
Manual render diagnostics are available with `bindport render --verbose`.
Automatic render diagnostics from `bindport run`, cleanup, or dashboard cleanup
are available with `BINDPORT_LOG=debug`; debug output avoids child environment
values and hook context payloads.

Custom templates are the extension point for file-based integrations. They can
feed TCP forwarder config, Kubernetes or IngressRoute manifests, container
helper input, env files, or JSON bridge files when another local tool owns the
actual watch, reload, apply, or forwarding behavior. BindPort should not be
treated as the TCP proxy, Kubernetes reconciler, Docker mutator, certificate
manager, DNS manager, or secret transport.

## Hooks

Hooks are declared in config but disabled until locally trusted with
`bindport hooks trust`. Hook trust is invalidated when a command definition or
local command target file changes. Dashboard hook actions are visibility-only;
trust decisions are CLI-only.

Supported hook events include `route_started`, `route_finished`,
`routes_removed`, `routes_marked_stale`, `render_requested`, and
`output_rendered`.

## Dashboard

The dashboard defaults to `127.0.0.1:27080`. Non-loopback binds require auth.
The dashboard shows active, reserved, stopped, and stale registry state; route
URLs; output status; health status; and hook trust visibility. It can clean
stopped and stale entries but cannot approve hooks.

## Agent Safety Rules

Agents should use existing project scripts or `bindport run <service>`, use
`bindport reserve --all` when all configured addresses are needed before
startup, run `bindport config validate` after config changes, discover ports
through `bindport port <service>`, discover URLs through
`bindport status --json` or `bindport open <service> --print`, and avoid editing
machine-local overrides or generated files unless explicitly asked. Agents
should not run hook trust, deny, reset, or hook commands without user approval.
